speaker: interviewer
text: It's December 7, 1995. Survivor is Gerda Klein. Last name at birth was Weissmann. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Scottsdale in the state of Arizona. Country is the United States of America. And the language is in English. My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is December 7, 1995. I am conducting an interview with survivor Gerda Klein. The interview is being conducted in Scottsdale, Arizona, in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sure? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Gerda Weissmann-Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-R-D-A. K-L-E-I-N. My maiden name is Weissmann-- was Weissmann. W-E-I-S-S-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, they called me Gertle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the border of Czechoslovakia and Poland, in a place called, in German, Bielitz. B-I-E-L-I-T-Z. And in Polish, when I was born, it was B-I-E-L-S-K-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe Bielitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielitz was a rather charming little town, surrounded by the Beskid mountain range. It-- it had a bit of an artistic flavor, since it had a castle of the Archduke Zhukovsky. It boasted a small theater-- outdoor concerts. But we were surrounded by mountains, and we did an awful lot of skiing and hiking in the summer. Lots of flowers, and of course in retrospect, it seems almost ideal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population when you were growing up, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was around 40,000. I'm really not quite sure, because it also had a sister city called Biala, B-I-A-L-A, and I don't know what the combined population of that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you say was the population of Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do believe there were about 4,000 Jews that I saw at, I think, in Yad Vashem, or-- but I really did not know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the town in which you grew up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was predominantly a textile manufacturer. As a matter of fact, they called it the Manchester central Europe. So a lot of people were engaged in this particular-- the manufacturer of textiles, and so on. And I think this is why, perhaps, living style was a little bit higher, because there were a lot of skilled workers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the home in which you grew up? Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Indeed I do. It was a very old home. My grandparents had married and moved there shortly after their marriage. My-- my mother was born there, and of course my brother and I were born there. It was a very old home, mostly stone. It had a very large garden with fruit trees and berry bushes and flowers, and-- and an attic to roam in, and a-- and a basement in which to play. And it was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Julius. And my mother's name was Helen. But it was Helene, actually, and she was called Lene.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your father. What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my father had-- had really studied medicine in-- in Vienna, before the First World War. When the war broke out, he was immediately thrown into the Austrian army, and I think he was totally discouraged by what-- by what he had to do. He said it was butchery. It was amputations and things like that, and not healing. And he never went back to it. He met my mother during the war. And then went into a family business-- not really our family, it was an extended family. And it was a production of furs, but not-- not for fashion. But rather for-- for the military and other commercial uses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you describe your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was tall, sort of reddish hair. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBBING] Very kind and special. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was obviously very close to my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He had endless patience, and-- and I think my father was rather unusual for a European father, you know. My friends' fathers were authority figures-- which my father was, too-- but I remember that, you know, whenever something was going on-- particularly, my brother and I used to put on plays-- my father would take off from his office, and come home to open the curtains and do things like that. Which, of course, contemporary fathers do. But a couple generations ago in Europe, I think that was rather rare. And my father had that special touch for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Helene, called Lene. She was not just by what I say, but she was sort of a renowned beauty. As a matter of fact. She had once won a contest in an awards ball in Vienna. She-- she was truly exquisitely beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And perhaps, you know, people used to say about me, she's such a beautiful mother, why does she look like her father? [LAUGHS] Which wasn't exactly good child psychology, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother really encompassed what, at that time, were all the womanly virtues, you know. She was a very accomplished hostess, and a marvelous cook and baker. And took great pride in all those things. And she wanted me to do that, as well. And, unfortunately, I really was not terribly good at it. You know, I preferred to roam and play with my cats. I had 10 cats. My brother had two dogs. And I used to love more the outdoors and climbing trees and running around. And, you know, I was probably much more my father's girl always, where my brother was extremely close to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would not say that. No. No, she was-- she used to love needlepoint, and spent an enormous amount of time doing that. Which, we, of course, as a child, could never understand it-- how, my father and my brother and I would go mountain climbing and things like that, my mother would be perfectly happy to be at home and embroider. I thought it was a terrible thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's maiden name was Muckenbrunn. It was rather unusual. It's M-U-- with an umlaut-- C-K-E-N-B-R-U-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an older brother. His name was Arthur. He looked very much like my mother. He was an extremely handsome boy. And it took me a long time until I realized that many of my girlfriends were sort of seeking my friendship in order to meet my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was also very close to my brother. I fought with him, of course, but-- but he was really an ideal older brother, you know. He fought my battles, and did things for me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was very accomplished. He was a very fine writer. He, as a matter of fact, if anyone still remembers him at a very young age, he wrote for a prestigious Polish paper called [POLISH]. which is a sort of a review of literature. And he wrote it under a-- under a pseudonym of Wera, W-E-- mean mean, capital W and a small E A-R, which spelled Arthur Weissmann together. And he did it when he was quite young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brother, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my paternal-- paternal grandmother quite well. She-- she died when I was about 13 years old. I don't remember my grandfather. He died when I was a year old. But I remember my maternal grandfather. I was four or five years old when he died. He died in the winter of 1929. And I just remember-- that-- it was terribly, terribly cold. And people came in probably from the funeral. That's probably one of the very few memories I have. But my grandmother, my maternal grandmother, we lived with her. She lived with us, and she died in 1938. I was 14 at the time. And of course I remember her very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Her name was Julia Muckenbrunn-- Julia Muckenbrunn. And she was a very authoritative woman. And she-- as a matter of fact, my other grandmother lived with us for-- for a while, too. So I was fortunate to have two grandmothers living with us. And, you know, in retrospect, I wonder, when people today talk about, you know, families have to separate, there was just no-- at least, you know, I think when I look back, it seems almost too ideal. And I wonder if it truly was as ideal, or if perhaps, that I have shut out in my memory things that might not have been. But it has served me well during some very difficult times. And I see no reason why-- why I should not keep those memories. There was a great age difference between my two grandmothers. My paternal grandmother could have been my other grandmother's mother, almost. So there was no discrepancy in age. But I thought of it this morning-- I think one of the most tender memories that I have, was, you know, in the morning. It was custom to get tea and milk in bed. And I remember my father carrying a tray for his mother, and my mother for her mother. And at times they would switch-- and-- with the others. And I think it was a beautiful thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think I was very fortunate to have experienced that with my-- with my grandmothers. Where, unfortunately, my children never knew their grandparents. And I think that's a terrible void. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended elementary school at first. And then I went to Catholic school. I went to a convent school called Notre Dame. It was taught by the sisters, by Franciscan sisters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, you know, we wore uniforms, and all that, and that was until I was 15. As a matter of fact, the war broke out just when I was just 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, attending a Catholic school and being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not a problem, because, you know, I-- I grew up in-- even though, you know, in an observant Jewish family, but my parents had many non-Jewish friends. And my Nana-- my nanny was the one who brought me up. She was Catholic. And as a matter of fact, I-- you know, she used to go to church every day. And as a small child, I used to go with her every day. And-- and so there was never any problem. I mean-- you know. I was taught to respect her religion, you know. And on Christmas we used to go to her house and I used to have help trim the tree and all that. And we had Hannukah. And there was great respect on both sides. And as a matter of fact, in-- in school, we used to have a rabbi coming to the convent to give us Jewish instructions, for which of course our parents paid separately. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was not too much of a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about your relationship with your-- with Nana. My Nana? Apparently that dated back a long time in my family, and Nana used to tell me that story-- that she was married to a postman in our town. And one day-- and he died very early. And a terrible fire broke out, which destroyed all her belongings. And the only thing that she had is her-- that was not destroyed was her husband's postman's jacket, which was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And she knew my grandmother slightly. And she came to my grandmother, you know, and everybody helped. You know, in those days, I guess there was no insurance or anything. So all the neighbors helped. And she had a granddaughter. It was a tragic thing. Her son died on the last day of World War I, and left a wife who went into service someplace. And she had a little granddaughter by the name of Irma. And she lived in a one room with Irma. And she came to see my grandmother. And my grandmother told her that my mother was expecting a baby, and so were looking for somebody to help for about a month or so. And she asked her if she would. And so she and Irma moved to our house, and they stayed for 13 years. And you know, my Nana was-- was very special to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm still very slightly in touch with her granddaughter, Irma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your religious background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Religion was a very important part in our home. We-- we had a kosher home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But my parents were, I guess, for its day and for Europe, quite liberal in their outlook. And I was taught to love my own religion and tradition, and to respect everybody else's religion, as well. We, of course, you know, Friday night was always a very special night with challah and with flowers, and with-- the family would come. You see, the home in which we lived, as I mentioned, my grandparents were living there. And both my grandparents were the oldest siblings of a large family. So the younger ones were more-- the other uncles and aunts of my mother were closer to her in age. And on Sunday, everybody would congregate in our house. It wasn't unusual to have more than 20 people on Sunday afternoon to come and to sit in the garden. As a matter of fact, the aunts kept comfortable clothes there, because people were wearing corsets and things like that. And they divested themselves of that, and-- and came. And the cousins, and so on. And-- and I think it really was-- it must have been, like, extensive psychology. I mean, everybody brought their problems there, and everything was being hashed and rehashed. And I must confess, I always listened to things. And they thought that I didn't understand. But I feel very fortunate that I know of many family things, because I was interested in it, and I wanted to know. And they thought I didn't know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a very large extended family. Even so, my mother only had one brother who left for Turkey around the time when I was a year or so old. I didn't see him often, like twice a year, he used to come back. But those uncles and aunts, which were my mother's uncles and aunts, but I called them uncles and aunts, and cousins, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But coming back to the religious situation, which-- which you wanted to know. My-- my father went to-- to temple every Saturday. Every Friday night and Saturday. And so Friday night, we had a large type of thing. We went, my brother and I, also went to temple every-- every Saturday afternoon. There were, sort of, children services. We actually belonged to two temples. You know, one was the more conservative one, where my parents were. But from school, we had to attend services which was in a-- well, you couldn't call it reform. You would call it conservative for American concept. So we were fairly closely tied to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Passover? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my God. My favorite holiday. It was wonderful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The excitement of bringing down the dishes, you know, which we sort of rediscovered every year. And some of the dishes dated back to my-- as a matter of fact, I do remember my great grandmother. And she was much more independent lady. So my grandmother lived with us after my grandfather's death. My maternal grandmother. My great grandmother was absolutely independent, and lived in her own apartment. And I would go with my grandmother to visit her mother. And she always gave me white cheese-- I don't know why, but that was something that she always gave me. And her name was Charlotte, but she was always called Great Grandmother Lotte, we used to call her. And her last name was Getreider. And I even know her maiden name was Fisher. And so we had some of the dishes, which dated back, probably, to the early 1980s-- you know. It was a wonderful discovery, when from the attic, we brought down, you know, and we had meat, and-- and dairy, of course, were separate. And all the things would come down. And all the excitement for the preparation of Passover. And my father would put on a long white type of gown, you know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was-- I was the youngest, so I had to ask the four questions, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the whole family was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sukkot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did build a sukkot, and we tried to eat outside. Most of the time it was too cold. You know, we lived very close to the mountains. And it could be cool. However, we decorated it each year, and friends would come. And my parents would invite their non-Jewish friends, and they would come, to drink some wine or some sweets and things like that. And we would go to the temple. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Purim?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would dress up for Purim. But I sort of just vaguely remember, perhaps, my parents, and some of my younger uncles and aunts doing it, but it was mostly a children's holiday. And we-- we did dress up for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other holidays that stand out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we hated Yom Kippur. You couldn't eat. You know, and I still hate not eating. But Hannukah, I think, was-- and Passover-- Passover's really my most favorite holiday. And Hannukah was also very, very beautiful, with the menorahs and things. And I'd like to tell you, later, a story about a menorah which I have, that I treasure very, very much. It is very special. And as a matter of fact, through an incredible miraculous way, I got about 400 family photographs, including my father's shabbos knife, with his writing on it, which I have. I'm very fortunate to have many things from my family home, where many of my friends don't have as much as, not only a photograph, but not a broken saucer that was in their home. And I was very fortunate to have a number of things which were in my parents' home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the Jewish community in Bielitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The community in Bielitz was, what, for its day, would probably be called reform. I would call it conservative, where the community of Biala, which was just across the river, was orthodox. And there was a lot of orthodox Jews there, and people would be wearing the traditional orthodox clothes. And perhaps I should mention that my father's family, who lived on the border of Romania and Russia, was very orthodox. And I used to visit there until my grandmother moved away from there. I was probably eight or nine, maybe 10. I used to visit there. And it was, in my memory, it remains like a portrait of Chagall, you know-- the market, and the horses, and the enormous sunflowers. And I would say that, emotionally, I feel much closer to my father's family. I think some of the legacy of the stories which my father talked about-- his father, who was an orthodox Jew. And I don't know if this is the time to tell you the story about him-- I think it deeply-- has influenced me, and I think that my own religious beliefs, and whatever spiritual substance I got-- I got from my father's family. Even so, you know, my mother's family was much more modern, and what was it-- I-- I do think that-- that there was a much deeper, much more spiritual thing in my father's family. And I still feel quite tied to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a youngster? What were you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I always wanted to write. And I always wanted to have all of my stories to have happy endings. All the orphans found their parents on some desert islands. I always wanted to be pretty. And I know I wasn't. I always wanted to be-- where my brother was very good looking, my mother was a renowned beauty, and I was always sort of a bit in the shadows. So I-- I tried to-- to overcompensate it in-- in a little, different ways. I was very shy. Painfully shy. I loved animals. There was always a lot of animals that I cared for, particularly cats. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I-- I think I had an extremely happy childhood. You know, there was so many people to go to. You know, I had two grandmothers for the most part of the thing, a great grandmother, my Nana, my mother. And of course, the center of my universe was my father. And-- so-- and my brother. You know, I think I've been extremely fortunate in that. And I think I felt quite-- quite comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your ambition, sort of? Your dreams of becoming in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: A writer. And a mother. Yeah. That's what I wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots. Mostly non-Jewish friends. And some terribly bitter disappointment. Particularly about my closest friend. And it's very strange. I couldn't talk about it until perhaps eight or nine years ago. I'd never written about it. So I've written about a lot things. There was a girl who was sort of my-- I consider her my alter ego. Her name also was Gerda. I had dark hair. She had blond hair. And we were closest friends. And I even sacrificed once going skiing with some kids whose brother I had a crush on, because she promised that day to come to my house and she did not ski. And I sort of suggested, you know, the greatest sacrifice to give it up for her. But she meant a great deal to me. And then, doing the war, one day, I happened to pass the post office wearing my yellow star as I saw her coming down the steps. And I sort of hushed, I says, Gerda, Gerda, you know. And she looked through me and she said, I don't talk to Jews. And it's very strange. I think that this probably was one of the most painful experiences that I have had. To be rejected in that fashion by someone who-- you know-- who meant a great deal to me. And it's very funny. I never talked about her. As a matter of fact, I had totally blocked it out of my mind, until some years ago, when someone I knew slightly got in touch with me. And I met her in Chicago. And she was going back to Poland. And she asked me if she could see somebody there. And I said, of course, my Nana has been dead a long time ago, but I told her to see my-- my granddaughter. And then she said to me, tell me how about Gerda Nickol-- that's the friend. When she said that name, it-- it absolutely-- I mean-- I-- I was just beside myself. And the strange thing happened-- and she said, could I write down the address of Nana's granddaughter. And when I did it, I found myself writing in exactly the same fashion as Gerda Nickol and I wrote. That name brought it totally back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and this is when it all came back. It was a devastating experience. And I feel rather bitter about it. Because when she came back, she told me that she inquired, and she found that Gerda Nickol-- when the Germans left our town, you know-- apparently went on to Germany. And I said, and I bet you she says that her closest friend was Jewish. And she tells the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to the occupation, did you experience any anti-Semitism personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. But you know, by what came later-- you know, you sort of knew, quote unquote, your place-- if you know what I mean. I remember, for instance, in school-- and that was in the convent school-- we-- twice a year, you-- you wrote an essay, which you didn't sign with your name, but with-- whatever, you know, a number, or what have you. And if you won, your essay was published in, sort of, a national scholastic, you would call it, magazine. And lo and behold, I won. And I remember-- and of course there was a great doing about it. And I remember, the next time around, my father took me for a walk. And he said, there's something I want to talk to you about. He said, the thing-- the contest is coming up. And you'd better not try too hard. It wouldn't do for a Jewish girl to win twice.
